questions.question: What does @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) do?
options: Creates the service immediately when the application starts
Registers the service with the root injector as an application-wide singleton
Makes the service available only to the root component
Prevents the service from being overridden in child modules

questions.question: What is the advantage of inject(UserService) over constructor injection?
options: inject() is faster because it skips TypeScript type checking
inject() works outside constructors (in property initializers, computed signals) and reduces constructor parameter noise
inject() creates a new service instance each time instead of using the singleton
inject() automatically unsubscribes from Observables on component destruction

questions.question: A UserService is provided in 'root'. How many instances exist in the application?
options: One per component that injects it
One per route that uses it
One for the entire application — singleton
One per NgModule that imports the service

questions.question: Why should components inject a UserService instead of HttpClient directly?
options: Angular restricts HttpClient to services only — components can't use it
HTTP logic in a service is reusable across components; in a component it's duplicated for each consumer
HttpClient requires zone.js which is not available in components
Services automatically retry failed requests; components don't

questions.question: How do you provide a service scoped to a single component and its children?
options: Use @Injectable({ providedIn: 'component' })
Add the service to the component's providers array: @Component({ providers: [MyService] })
Use inject(MyService, { self: true })
Call injector.get(MyService) inside the component's constructor
